data.title: Bitcoin 2011: Out of Control
data.description:
Author: Liu Honglin

On June 19, 2011, a Bitcoin player sat in front of his computer, repeatedly refreshing the Mt. Gox trading page.

The light from the screen fell on his face, but the page failed to provide a reassuring answer. A few months earlier, a Bitcoin had just touched $1; by June, it had surged to over ten dollars. In forums, some were calculating wealth, some were discussing mining, and some were beginning to seriously study exchanges and wallets. A small experiment that originally belonged to cryptography mailing lists and geek forums suddenly had a price that could keep ordinary people up at night.

That night, he wasn't looking at an abstract market chart.

He saw his own account, the orders pending on the exchange, the imagination ignited by the price, and the primal panic of a young market suddenly spiraling out of control.

Bitcoin, which had been hovering around $17 just moments before, plummeted to a few cents within minutes. Some thought the website was malfunctioning, some kept refreshing, some rushed to change their passwords, and some stared blankly at the transaction records. Chat rooms and forums quickly erupted into a cacophony of voices: Had accounts been hacked? Was the exchange down? Could orders be cancelled? Were the coins still valid? Even more unsettling was that the Bitcoin network hadn't stopped. Blocks continued to move forward at their own pace, transactions could still be confirmed, and the protocol, like a machine oblivious to human panic, operated quietly. What broke down was the world outside the protocol: exchange accounts, databases, passwords, administrator privileges, and the fragile trust that users had just built. At that moment, early adopters saw clearly for the first time: Bitcoin claims it doesn't need banks, yet people still entrust their coins to some website; it claims it doesn't need trusted third parties, yet price, liquidity, and access will inevitably push new third parties to the center stage. This new third party had no bank lobby, no tellers, no deposit insurance, and no mature auditing system; it only had a website and a group of tech people who weren't yet ready to take on financial risks. In 2010, those two pizzas made Bitcoin resemble money for the first time. In 2011, it began to bear the weight of money. Price fueled greed, the black market fueled demand, the media fueled public scrutiny, and charitable organizations raised concerns. Everything began to remind people that anonymity, wealth, and security never automatically materialize simply by being written into code. If one had to describe Bitcoin's year 2011 in one word, it would be: out of control. This out-of-control situation wasn't a protocol collapse. Rather, it was the simultaneous onslaught of all the old problems after a new species prematurely entered the real world. Bitcoin, having just emerged from a small technical forum, encountered philanthropic organizations, The New Yorker, Silk Road, Mt. Gox, hackers, senators, and a group of ordinary people dreaming of getting rich quick. This wasn't Bitcoin's most glorious year, nor can it be dismissed with a simple "dark history." It was more like the crypto world's first coming-of-age ceremony: a young system still in an engineer's coat was thrust onto a stage where finance, law, crime, public opinion, and commercial infrastructure were all under scrutiny, forced to answer what it truly was. In January, philanthropic organizations tested the waters. On January 20, 2011, the Electronic Frontier Foundation published an article on its website with the gist of: Bitcoin is a step towards censorship-resistant digital currencies. The Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF) is a US-based non-profit organization focused on digital rights, privacy, and internet freedom. Its perspective on Bitcoin differs from that of traders. While traders primarily ask how much a single Bitcoin can appreciate, the EFF is more concerned with whether individuals can retain a degree of autonomy akin to cash in an era where online payments increasingly rely on banks, credit cards, PayPal, Visa, Mastercard, and regulatory interfaces. This article needs to understand Bitcoin within the context of payment blockades following WikiLeaks. In late 2010, payment institutions such as PayPal, Visa, and Mastercard cut off donations to WikiLeaks. For many technocrats, this served as a reminder that while the internet allows information to flow rapidly, money remains tied up in the hands of a few financial intermediaries. When payment gateways are shut down, an organization can be commercially suffocated. Bitcoin appears very attractive in this context. It doesn't require bank approval or merchant account activation from payment companies; users can send and receive messages directly after downloading the software. The EFF acknowledges that this design addresses privacy and autonomy concerns, and also mentions that Bitcoin uses public transaction records to prevent double-spending, no longer relying on a single third-party ledger. However, this article isn't blindly enthusiastic. It also cautions that Bitcoin's anonymity is not robust and may raise legal issues related to money laundering, taxation, and consumer protection. In other words, by January 2011, those who first seriously recognized Bitcoin's public value had already foreseen its legal troubles. The subsequent crypto industry has almost always lived within this tension. The same tools can be a gateway to bypass payment blockades for donors, a channel to circumvent financial monitoring for law enforcement, and a matter of reputation and legal liability for charitable organizations. The EFF saw Bitcoin's public value early on, and also its troubles early on. At the beginning of 2011, Bitcoin hadn't truly broken out of its niche. EFF has moved it from a programmer's toy into public discourse on payment oversight, privacy, financial intermediation, and legal liability. That door opened a crack first with the help of non-profit organizations. In February, Bitcoin reached one dollar. On February 10, 2011, the price of Bitcoin reached $1. In 2009, the Neoliberal Standard was still using electricity costs to value Bitcoin, as if searching for the minimum cost explanation for a strange species; in 2010, Laszlo bought two pizzas with 10,000 Bitcoins, finally giving Bitcoin a story of exchange that could be told to ordinary people. By February 2011, the US dollar entered the fray, and the way the discussion shifted. People stopped asking simply whether it was usable and started asking how much it was worth, and were more concerned about whether it would be more expensive tomorrow. Once the question becomes price, people change. Those who previously ran the client might have been cryptography enthusiasts, open-source users, or believers in free money. With prices emerging, newcomers asked more direct questions: Is it expensive to buy now? Will it rise tomorrow? Where can I buy it? Who can sell it? Where can I store it after buying? Can I get my money back when I withdraw? Bitcoin initially aimed to prove that the ledger could function without banks and payment companies. With the market entering the fray, things immediately became less pure. Without a trusted third party, why would users put their coins on exchanges? Despite its supposedly peer-to-peer nature, why were more and more people flocking to websites like Mt. Gox? Without bank accounts, why did people still want to exchange Bitcoin back for US dollars? The first irony appears here as well. Bitcoin aims to reduce old intermediaries, but as soon as a price appears, new intermediaries immediately emerge. They aren't called banks, they lack deposit insurance, mature auditing, and significant regulatory oversight, yet users have begun entrusting them with their assets, accounts, passwords, and trust. One dollar brought Bitcoin to the market's attention, but also brought in an even more chaotic set of market elements. In March, Mt. Gox changed hands. In the spring of 2011, Mt. Gox changed hands. The name Mt. Gox comes from "Magic: The Gathering Online Exchange," originally related to online card trading for Magic: The Gathering. Founder Jed McCaleb, an American programmer, had worked on peer-to-peer file-sharing networks like eDonkey. He later transformed this old domain into a Bitcoin trading portal: what was originally a place for card enthusiasts to exchange currency became the most important price window in the early Bitcoin world. At the time, the Mt. Gox page already performed several functions: providing exchange rates between USD and BTC, accepting user registration and deposits, offering buy and sell orders, and promising users the ability to withdraw their funds. For early adopters, the website might not have resembled a financial institution, but in practice, it had become a price board, a trading desk, and an asset portal. Around March, Jed McCaleb handed Mt. Gox over to Mark Karpelès, a French developer living in Japan. Later, the Chinese-speaking community called Mt. Gox "Mentougou," a translation with a touch of unexpected absurdity. Ironically, it perfectly captured the exchange's fate: a narrow entrance, a steep road, yet it swallowed too much money, trust, and panic over the years. The true significance of this handover wasn't just that two programmers completed a website transaction, but that it prematurely exposed a recurring problem the crypto industry would later face: a system claiming to need no banks will quickly spawn its own "quasi-banks"; a network claiming peer-to-peer will quickly re-centralize trust around exchanges, wallets, custody, and payment gateways. Users can't forever find buyers, safeguard their private keys, and handle fiat currency inflows and outflows themselves. Convenience will lead them to platforms, and platforms will bring back security, compliance, risk control, and accountability issues. The June incident wasn't a disaster that fell from the sky. Its foreshadowing was already written this spring: a new market was growing too fast, while the infrastructure that initially supported it was still stuck in the era of personal websites. In April, a new species emerged. On April 18, 2011, in the "Alternate cryptocurrencies" section of BitcoinTalk, user vinced posted a thread titled: Namecoin, a distributed naming system based on Bitcoin. This post itself resembled the release notes of an early open-source project. It lacked commercial packaging; the main text was a PGP signature. vinced stated that Namecoin was a naming system modified from Bitcoin, inspired by previous discussions about BitDNS and the recent failures and controversies surrounding traditional DNS systems. It wasn't simply adding a feature to the Bitcoin main chain, but rather creating a new blockchain separate from Bitcoin. The problem Namecoin aims to solve can be understood starting with "names." Domain names, accounts, and identities on the internet are typically managed by centralized institutions. Namecoin envisions binding names and values ​​together and writing them into the blockchain. A name is acquired through a new transaction type, and after registration, it requires an initial update and subsequent updates; if it is not renewed within 12,000 blocks, the name expires; two names that have not yet expired cannot be duplicated. The initially defined namespace included DNS and personal, with DNS pointing to a distributed top-level domain, possibly called .bit. The post also listed what the system truly lacked at the time: someone needed to compile and run namecoind, someone needed to participate in mining, and proxies, browser plugins, and DNS servers were needed to connect the chain to the internet experience of ordinary users. In other words, Namecoin didn't just invent a concept; from day one, it exposed the old problems of blockchain applications: on-chain rules can be written, but off-chain entry points, user tools, and infrastructure still need to be built separately. Namecoin never became the gateway to the mainstream internet, and .bit domains never truly entered the daily lives of ordinary users. However, its significance in 2011 remains clear: Bitcoin's code and mechanisms began to be used to solve problems beyond currency. Proof-of-work, distributed ledgers, public verification, and tamper-proof state records were extracted from "electronic cash" and became prototypes that other blockchain projects could borrow. Later projects like Litecoin, domain coins, anonymity coins, smart contract platforms, on-chain identity, NFTs, DeFi, and RWA will all continue to explore similar questions: Is blockchain merely for recording money, or can it be used to record names, identities, assets, and rights relationships?

In April, Satoshi Nakamoto withdrew from daily communication.

Also in April, Satoshi Nakamoto withdrew from daily communication.

On December 12, 2010, at 6:22 PM GMT, Satoshi Nakamoto left his last public post on the Bitcoin forum, containing only some details about the new software. After that, his email replies became unstable. On April 26, 2011, Gavin Andresen told other developers that Satoshi Nakamoto had advised him that morning, and the entire development team, to downplay the "mysterious founder" when publicly discussing Bitcoin. Subsequently, Satoshi Nakamoto even stopped replying to emails from Gavin.

The New Yorker later quoted Satoshi Nakamoto as saying that he had told the developers he had moved on to other things. From that moment on, Bitcoin's code continued to run, development continued, and the media and government agencies continued to question what it really was, but the person who initially wrote the system and was most likely to be seen as the "person in charge" by the outside world was no longer there. In June, the Silk Road. On June 1, 2011, Wired's Threat Level column published the Silk Road report, which has since been repeatedly mentioned. The title was straightforward: An underground website that lets you buy almost any drug. The report began with a software developer using the pseudonym Mark. He found a seller with good feedback on Silk Road, added LSD to his cart, clicked checkout, filled in his shipping address, and paid 50 bitcoins, which was about $150 at the time. Four days later, the goods arrived at his home in Canada. This detail transformed Silk Road from an abstract underground website into a reproducible transaction process: product selection, user reviews, ordering, payment, and shipping. Silk Road required access via Tor. Tor can be understood as an anonymous access network that forwards user traffic through multiple layers of relays, making it difficult for the website and external observers to directly see the visitor's real web address. Silk Road did not accept credit cards, PayPal, or other payment methods that are easily tracked, frozen, or charged; it only accepted bitcoins. Anonymous access networks, bitcoin payments, seller review systems, and postal delivery combined to form one of the most impactful underground e-commerce models of 2011. This report changed Bitcoin's public image. Previously, Bitcoin could be interpreted as a cypherpunk experiment, libertarian electronic cash, programmer's open-source toy, and a reflection on currency after the financial crisis. After Silk Road went viral, mainstream society recognized it for the first time in a more jarring way: if an underground drug market only accepts Bitcoin, is Bitcoin a free currency or a tool for criminal settlement? Silk Road is certainly not the whole story of Bitcoin, nor is it the result that Bitcoin's designers necessarily intended. However, from a payment perspective, it almost completely demonstrates another side of Bitcoin: cross-border, not easily frozen, addresses not directly linked to real identities, able to integrate with anonymous networks, and suitable for transactions between parties without traditional merchant accounts. Technological neutrality has encountered real-world consequences here. A payment network that doesn't discriminate based on the purpose of a transaction can serve dissident organizations as well as the black market. It can protect donors from being arbitrarily blocked by payment institutions, and make it more difficult for law enforcement to trace transactions along traditional account systems. It offers some a glimpse of freedom, while others see it as a loss of control. Following this report, the relationship between Bitcoin and Silk Road quickly entered public discussion. Wired's November review noted that after Gawker/Wired's report on Silk Road, the price of Bitcoin surged within a week. The reason is simple: if an underground market only accepts Bitcoin, those wanting to enter must first buy Bitcoin. Meanwhile, political pressure also began to emerge. US Senators Charles Schumer and Joe Manchin publicly called on law enforcement to crack down on Silk Road. Schumer, in a press conference, described Silk Road as a rampant online drug trafficking attempt and Bitcoin as a form of online money laundering. For a relatively young open-source currency project, this label spreads faster and is more likely to remain in the public's mind than technical explanations in forums. Silk Road brought Bitcoin its first use case that mainstream media couldn't ignore, but it also brought a reputational burden that was difficult to shake off. It proved that Bitcoin could complete settlements outside of the traditional payment system, but at the same time, it pushed Bitcoin into the context of drug trafficking, money laundering, and law enforcement concerns. A problem that the crypto industry has repeatedly encountered since 2011 was already beginning to take shape here: real demand does not naturally equal legitimate demand, and the fact that a transaction can occur does not mean that society will accept the way it occurs. In June, the EFF hit the brakes. After Silk Road propelled Bitcoin into the public eye, the hand that had reached out first in January began to withdraw. On June 20, 2011, the EFF announced on its official website that it would no longer accept Bitcoin donations. This action isn't a simple "change of heart." More accurately, it's like an organization deciding, after a meeting in its office, to remove a hot new button from its website. The article clearly states: EFF had been monitoring Bitcoin for several months and even tried accepting Bitcoin donations in an account set up for it by someone else. However, it later removed this donation option from its official help page. There are three reasons for this. First, the legal issues are too uncertain. Securities law, taxation, consumer protection, and money laundering issues still lack clear answers. EFF can defend users of new technologies, but it doesn't want to become the one testing the boundaries of the law. Second, how to spend the donations is also complicated. Donors give money hoping that charitable organizations will use it to support their work; but if the conversion of Bitcoin into cash is unclear, it will be difficult for the organization to explain. Third, it didn't want to create a misunderstanding: the EFF's acceptance of Bitcoin didn't equate to the EFF endorsing Bitcoin, much less implying that Bitcoin was safe, stable, or without investment risk. Most interestingly, the EFF didn't sell the received coins or return them to donors; instead, it decided to put them into the Bitcoin faucet, allowing them to continue circulating within the community. Many years later, companies, foundations, universities, merchants, listed companies, and payment institutions will face similar questions: Can cryptocurrencies be accepted? How should they be recorded on asset statements? How should they be valued in accounting? How can they be converted into fiat currency? These questions were already beginning to take shape with the EFF in 2011. Bitcoin hadn't even become a financial asset yet, and legal issues were already catching up. In June, Mt. Gox shut down. Silk Road brought Bitcoin into the media spotlight, and Mt. Gox dragged it back into the spotlight. On June 19, 2011, the price of Mt. Gox plummeted from around $17 to a few cents. Wired reported that day that the exchange suspended trading and directed visitors to a statement claiming the incident stemmed from a compromised account. Subsequently, user databases were leaked, and usernames, emails, and hashed passwords circulated online. The chain of events exposed by the incident was not complex. Mt. Gox was one of the main gateways for exchanging US dollars and Bitcoin at the time, and users entrusted their accounts, passwords, order books, and assets to the website. After the attack, the trading price plummeted on the platform, the exchange suspended services, and administrators attempted to roll back suspicious transactions. Subsequently, the user database was leaked, and usernames, email addresses, and hashed passwords began circulating online. The problem wasn't with the underlying Bitcoin ledger, but with the exchange system that users relied on to access the market. Mt. Gox eventually recovered in 2011, but it didn't truly cure its problems. In 2014, it collapsed in a more devastating way, entering bankruptcy proceedings, with many users waiting for compensation and liquidation for years. This story later leads to the tale of Jesse Powell and Kraken: Powell traveled to Japan to assist with the crisis management after the 2011 Mt. Gox incident and subsequently began building an exchange with a more central focus on security controls. Coinbase, after 2012, attempted to answer another question with a more internet-product-oriented approach: could ordinary people buy, sell, and store Bitcoin like they would a bank app? These companies are certainly not the simple opposite of Mt. Gox. They later encountered new problems such as regulation, custody, compliance, listing, user asset segregation, and market manipulation. However, from the perspective of industry evolution, the Mt. Gox incident did leave a shadow on later entrepreneurs: exchanges cannot simply be websites that facilitate buying and selling; they must handle security, risk control, auditing, customer assets, abnormal transactions, and external supervision like financial institutions. Many years later, cold and hot wallets, multi-signature permissions, internal approval, proof of reserves, compliance officers, on-chain risk control, judicial investigation, and custody licenses will become common terms in the industry. Users in 2011 didn't have these terms. They simply learned a basic truth on a bad night: the coins are on-chain, but that doesn't mean the risk is only on-chain. In June, 25,000 coins were stolen. Beyond exchanges, personal wallets also began to experience problems. In mid-June 2011, an early user named Allinvain posted on BitcoinTalk. The title was short but impactful: "I've just been hacked, any help is welcome, 25,000 BTC stolen." The imagery in the post was even more painful than the numbers. He said he woke up to find a large amount of Bitcoin had been transferred from his wallet to an unknown address. He tried to restore an earlier backup, but the transactions had already been confirmed. He suspected his computer or mining pool account had been compromised first, and then his wallet file was taken. The most heartbreaking part was roughly: "I meticulously backed up wallet.dat and encrypted it, but if someone has directly accessed my computer, all those efforts are useless." At the time, this was a loss in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. Wired later cautiously noted that while the veracity of this claim cannot be fully confirmed, it has certainly become one of the most representative stories in the early security history of Bitcoin. It made many people realize for the first time that owning their own money doesn't necessarily mean greater ease. Traditional bank accounts certainly have centralized problems. Accounts can be frozen, transactions can be audited, banks can make mistakes, and user privacy can be shared. But the banking system also provides some security buffers that ordinary people are already accustomed to: password recovery, abnormal transaction monitoring, customer service appeals, judicial freezing, account loss reporting, and risk control compensation. Bitcoin returns much power to users, but also pushes many responsibilities onto them. Your private key is in your hands, your assets are in your hands; if you lose your private key, your assets may be lost forever; a computer virus, copied wallet files, a weak password, or inadequate backups—the consequences are no longer just "changing your password." This system doesn't have a natural customer service center waiting to bail you out. Bitcoin's self-custodial ideal began to become burdensome in 2011. In the past, a wallet file was simply a file on a hard drive; after the price rose, it became like a gold bar without a safe. More troublesome, the problem wasn't limited to personal computers. Wired reviewed several early incidents: Bitomat in Poland, once the third-largest exchange, lost approximately 17,000 bitcoins after overwriting wallet files; the established wallet service MyBitcoin experienced prolonged outages, causing user panic. MyBitcoin later claimed it was hacked, but for users at the time, the more immediate problem was: I kept my coins in an online wallet, and now the provider isn't replying to emails. The problem with old finance was users' over-reliance on banks and payment institutions; the problem with new finance was users' belief that after getting rid of banks, any webpage could replace them. Bitcoin users in 2011 oscillated between these two extremes. On one end is self-custody, where you keep your private key and have no one to cover your losses if something goes wrong; on the other end is escrow, which is more convenient, but entrusts your trust to a website that may lack qualifications, auditing, regulation, or even transparency regarding your real identity. Back then, people didn't have the mature terms they would later use: cold and hot wallets, multi-signature permissions, escrow licenses, audit reports, on-chain risk control. Users only knew one thing: Bitcoin was truly valuable, but they didn't know how to safeguard it. In July, the myth of anonymity was debunked. In July 2011, a paper titled "An Analysis of Anonymity in the Bitcoin System" was published on arXiv. arXiv is a preprint platform for academic papers. This article is by Fergal Reid and Martin Harrigan. The two authors were from the Clique Research Cluster and Complex & Adaptive Systems Laboratory at University College Dublin, Ireland, and their research focused on network architecture, complex systems, and information network analysis. The paper points out that Bitcoin users identify themselves using public keys, and attackers can attempt to reconstruct the relationship between users and public keys. They also analyzed a Bitcoin theft incident, at the time valued at approximately $500,000, by combining on-chain structure and external information. The core conclusion of the paper is not complex: Bitcoin is not an absolutely anonymous system, but rather closer to a pseudonymous system on a public ledger. While addresses themselves don't display real names, anonymity becomes fragile once transaction relationships, fund flows, timelines, exchange deposits and withdrawals, forum posts, donation records, and real-world identities are linked to external information. This contrasts sharply with the story of the Silk Road. Black market users believed they were hiding behind anonymous access networks and Bitcoin addresses, but researchers saw another side: on-chain records don't disappear, and fund flows can be observed long-term. Traditional cash transactions are difficult to trace once completed; Bitcoin transactions, once on-chain, remain on the public ledger for a long time. Later, on-chain analytics companies, exchange compliance departments, law enforcement agencies, and judicial investigation services developed along this path. This 2011 paper was still just academic analysis, but it clearly stated a crucial fact: Bitcoin's anonymity isn't implicit; it depends on how users use addresses, exchanges, network tools, and real-world identities. If these clues are connected, the path of money can re-emerge as a personal story. In 2011, the story was just beginning. In October, mainstream media took notice. In October 2011, *The New Yorker* published Joshua Davis's long article, "The Crypto-Currency." This article brought Bitcoin into the narrative framework of mainstream American magazines. It not only introduced "what Bitcoin is," but also brought together several clues that had already surfaced at the time: Who is Satoshi Nakamoto? Can the code withstand scrutiny from security researchers? Why are merchants willing to accept Bitcoin? Legally, should it be considered currency, a commodity, or a security? The first clue was the search for Satoshi Nakamoto. Journalist Joshua Davis tracked down candidates like Michael Clear and Vili Lehdonvirta, but ultimately found no definitive answer. Michael Clear was young at the time, knowledgeable in cryptography, and had commented on the elegance and limitations of Bitcoin's design; Vili Lehdonvirta explicitly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto. This investigation, though inconclusive, made the "anonymous founder" a central mystery in Bitcoin's mainstream narrative. The second line of investigation is code scrutiny. The New Yorker reported that security researcher Dan Kaminsky attempted to find attack points in Bitcoin's code. Kaminsky was not an ordinary user; he was a well-known internet security researcher. He initially thought he could find vulnerabilities, but discovered that many attack paths had already been blocked by Satoshi Nakamoto. For Bitcoin, this kind of scrutiny meant it was no longer just a theoretical experiment in forums, but began to be publicly examined by external security experts. The third line of investigation is its legal and commercial attributes. The article mentions that some law professors believe Bitcoin exists in a gray area, unsure whether it should be considered currency, a commodity, or potentially a security; some merchants use Bitcoin as a payment tool to circumvent credit card fees and chargeback risks. By October 2011, Bitcoin was no longer just a tech company. The media, merchants, academics, security researchers, and regulators all began to treat it as a new financial phenomenon requiring explanation. In November, the price receded. Looking back at Bitcoin that year in November 2011, the price curve was no longer just a story of rising prices. Wired, in a lengthy article in November, recalled that from early April to the end of May 2011, the price of Bitcoin rose from less than $1 to $8.89; after the Silk Road report in early June, it surged again within a week, briefly approaching $27. The New Yorker's record shows an even higher peak: a single Bitcoin exceeded $29 in June, but by September it had fallen to $5. Wired, in that review, also wrote that the gold rush mining phase was ending, with some miners selling off modified mining rigs due to unbearable electricity costs, noise, and heat; meanwhile, those who remained more serious began to focus on infrastructure. Mt. Gox was researching point-of-sale hardware, other entrepreneurs were trying to create online merchant services similar to PayPal, and someone in Colorado created BitcoinDeals, aiming to sell millions of items using Bitcoin. After the price drop, what remained wasn't just losses and arguments, but also a group of people starting to push Bitcoin beyond the price narrative towards the more mundane aspects of payments, merchants, exchanges, and wallets. The 2011 downturn didn't push Bitcoin back to the obscure experiment of 2009. It has been rewritten simultaneously by the media, the black market, academics, exchanges, NGOs, and ordinary users. By the end of 2011, it was difficult to explain Bitcoin in a single sentence. It was like a post-financial crisis critique of the old monetary system, a settlement tool in the internet black market; like an open-source experiment by programmers, and a price game by speculators; it captivated digital rights organizations, yet quickly made them realize the legal implications. Many years later, the crypto industry will invent a whole host of mature terms: exchanges, wallets, mining pools, stablecoins, DeFi, RWA, on-chain risk control, custody licenses, travel rules, coin mixing sanctions, judicial investigations. But the old problems behind these terms had already surfaced in 2011. Bitcoin removes the ledger from institutional trust and entrusts it to a public network, cryptography, and consensus rules. But it doesn't remove people from the real world. People are still greedy, careless, speculative, and panicky; institutions still make mistakes, the media still amplifies conflicts, and governments still hold people accountable. Code can keep the ledger moving forward, but it can't suddenly make everyone working around the ledger reliable. Therefore, what's most memorable about 2011 isn't just Bitcoin reaching one dollar, nor just its rise to fame on the black market, nor just its first stumble at Mt. Gox. The bigger change is that Bitcoin, for the first time, simultaneously exists in two worlds: one world focused on hashes, private keys, blocks, computing power, and the longest chain; the other world focused on exchanges, drugs, donations, law enforcement, password leaks, user losses, and public opinion. In 2008, Satoshi Nakamoto proposed an electronic cash system that didn't require a trusted third party. In 2011, the real world gave it a harsher, but more truthful, answer: ledgers can have fewer intermediaries, but people will constantly create new intermediaries, thus testing trust once again. Bitcoin's 2011 quietly passed, leaving behind only two words: out of control.

Trump Earns $2.2 Billion in 2025, Large Proportion from Cryptocurrency Sector A financial document released on Tuesday, July 1st, reveals that US President Donald Trump's total income in 2025 will reach at least $2.2 billion, a significant increase from his minimum income of $622 million before his return to the White House in 2024. A substantial proportion of this new income comes from the cryptocurrency sector. Trump is the co-founder of his family business, World Liberty Financial, which has already generated $799 million in revenue, partly from funds purchased by the UAE government for its shares. Three days before his inauguration in January 2025, Trump also participated in the launch of the meme coin "$TRUMP". The financial disclosure shows that this project contributed $636 million in revenue, slightly higher than the total revenue from all other businesses in 2024. AI Hot Topics 1. Meta is selling its surplus AI computing resources Market News: Meta Platforms (META.O) is building a cloud business to sell its surplus AI computing resources. 2. Together AI announces completion of $800 million funding round, latest valuation reaches $8.3 billion On July 1, Together AI announced the completion of an $800 million funding round, with its latest valuation reaching $8.3 billion.

3. National Grid Invests $1.75 Billion in US AI Power Company

On July 1st, National Grid Plc, a London-listed power grid company with a large electricity and gas business in the northeastern United States, is taking a significant step to capitalize on the booming US data center industry by acquiring a stake in a company developing power generation facilities for Microsoft (MSFT.US). The utility will invest $1.75 billion to acquire a 35% stake in Joulent LLC. Joulent LLC is partnering with oil giant Chevron (CVX.US) to develop a natural gas power plant for Microsoft in Texas. Energy companies are broadly seeking investment opportunities in artificial intelligence through data center power projects. This deal values ​​the privately held company Joulent at $5 billion and will expand National Grid's business footprint from the northeastern United States to one of the hottest electricity markets in the country.

data.title: From Circle to OUSD: The Offense and Defense of the Stablecoin Race
data.description: In the past two days, a piece of news has caused some ripples in both the crypto ecosystem and the US stock market ecosystem. It is understood that the deal is a joint effort between payment giant Stripe/Tempo, credit card giant Visa, Mastercard, asset management giant BlackRock, and major cryptocurrencies. Coinbase and over 140 companies jointly launched the Open Standard consortium and introduced a new stablecoin, Open USD. This news has sparked numerous comments on X, with many believing that Circle's position is threatened by this organization and OUSD. In a previous Q&A article, I shared my thoughts on Circle: I wasn't entirely confident about its business model, so I've never bought its stock. To this day, I still haven't bought its stock, but I've maintained a certain level of interest in it. So, after seeing this news, I took the opportunity to reorganize my thoughts. Although I haven't bought its stock and still have many questions about it, I don't think this new stablecoin launched by such an alliance will ultimately have much impact on Circle's status. My reasons are mainly twofold: The first reason is a rule of thumb I've always believed in: In a new ecosystem, the ultimate top players are almost always native startup teams from that ecosystem, and very few are veteran players from the old world. In the stablecoin sector, who are the native startup teams? In my opinion, the three strongest are: USDT, USDC, and DAI/USDS. However, it's also possible that new original teams will emerge in the future. Besides these, things like Stripe, MasterCard, etc., all belong to the old players of the old world. They are likely to be constrained by "experience" and create some awkward or incongruous things. The issuance of stablecoins is not technically difficult; the difficulty lies in their use and promotion. Such use and promotion require a unique cryptographic quality—something that veteran players often lack, or even, frankly, completely lack. Therefore, the threat posed by Circle is unlikely to come from anything created by these veteran player alliances. The second reason is that, generally speaking, most of these alliances are all talk and no action. They appear to come from different industries, have different needs, and are capable of creating something more widely accepted. This background is an advantage in terms of legislation, but often a disadvantage in the business world, because such loose alliances are rarely able to effectively drive commercial implementation. The likely future scenario for OUSD is: there will be application scenarios and implementation, but it will be difficult to shake the position of USDC. Therefore, this news can actually be completely ignored. I think that besides some points about Circle's business model that I don't quite understand, another thing I have reservations about is its team. The founding team of Circle is undoubtedly excellent, but they are still quite different from what I imagined. This might stem from a certain obsession of mine. The crypto ecosystem is a completely new world, a world capable of providing unique conditions for the birth of historically great companies. Therefore, I always look forward to seeing some remarkable individuals and companies emerge from this ecosystem. What constitutes "remarkable" is not easy to define, but what constitutes "not so remarkable" is quite easy to discern. What makes me think the Circle team isn't "that great"? It's that they've issued their own blockchain layer and want to create their own payment chain. In the crypto ecosystem, the easiest thing is to issue a coin, and the second easiest is to build a blockchain. Is issuing a coin acceptable? Of course, if needed, it should be done; but if it's not needed at all, does the team have the courage to hold back and not issue a coin? Similarly, is it possible to build a blockchain? Of course, it is; if needed, a blockchain should be built. But if it's not needed at all, does the team have the courage to resist the temptation not to build a blockchain? In the stablecoin field, one easily conceived path is to build one's own blockchain because "stablecoins need payment channels," so building a blockchain becomes a "natural" thing to do. Stripe has its own chain, Plasma is also a chain... But in my opinion, most of these blockchains claiming to be dedicated to the payment sector will prove to be useless in the future. Saying they are "useless" doesn't mean they are completely worthless, but rather that many of them will be used, but their market share will be very small. Veteran players from the old world entering this arena can easily fall into the trap of blockchain development. If a core team doesn't even recognize this pitfall and jumps in anyway, it indicates the team's fundamental capabilities are somewhat mediocre. For a company, when it has ample resources and resources, doing what it wants is often easy, but resisting temptations is often extremely difficult. Unfortunately, Circle couldn't escape the common pitfalls. So, in my eyes, it's not that impressive. I don't fully understand its business model, and its corporate culture and characteristics aren't particularly appealing to me, so I've remained an observer.

data.title: The 30 million yuan investment in Suzhou 18 years ago has become the lifeblood of AI.
data.description:
Article by Sleepy and Daria

Suzhou's most profitable investment occurred during the global financial crisis.

In the autumn of 2008, Lehman Brothers collapsed, and venture capital in China almost came to a standstill. Some cut their losses, others waited and saw, and the primary market entered a frozen period. It was during that window that Yuanhe Holdings, a state-owned venture capital platform in Suzhou Industrial Park, invested approximately 30 million RMB in a startup.

The founder was Liu Sheng, a Tsinghua University graduate with a PhD from Georgia Institute of Technology. He had worked in optical communications in North America for many years, first at Lucent and then at Opnext.

In 2008, he returned from Silicon Valley and registered Accelink Technologies in Suzhou, aiming to develop high-speed optical modules. In the investment world of 2008, optical modules were far from a niche market; almost no investors were paying attention to this sector. Domestic optical communication companies were all crammed into the telecom market and low-to-mid-end manufacturing. According to a LightCounting report, in 2010, only one Chinese company, WTD, was among the top ten global optical module manufacturers. WTD was later merged into Accelink Technologies, barely making it to the bottom of the list. At that time, the assertion that "cloud computing data centers need a large number of high-speed optical modules" was far from any consensus. Eighteen years later, this investment has grown into Accelink Technologies. Before the Dragon Boat Festival in June 2026, its stock price surged to 1367.88 yuan, with a total market capitalization of 1.5 trillion yuan, placing it among the top ten A-share companies, surpassing Kweichow Moutai. The Blood Vessels of AI Optical modules are crucial to the entire AI industry. Without them, AI is just a bunch of hot silicon chips. Signals leaving the chip need optical modules to be transmitted between data centers via fiber optics. Computing power is the heart, and optical modules are the blood vessels. A-share listed companies such as Xin Yisheng, Zhongji Xuchuang, and Tianfu Communication are collectively known as "Yi Zhongtian." These three optical module giants control the most critical segment of the global AI computing power supply chain. Two of these companies are headquartered in Suzhou: Zhongji Xuchuang's Suzhou operations headquarters are in Suzhou Industrial Park, and Tianfu Communication's operations headquarters are also there. In addition, Suzhou-based Lianxun Instruments, a manufacturer of high-end optical communication testing instruments, saw its stock price rise more than twenty times from its IPO price in less than two months after listing in 2026, becoming the top-performing stock in the A-share market. Yuanjie Technology, a leading active optical chip manufacturer and a company in the supply chain of Zhongji Xuchuang Capital, saw its stock price reach 1712 yuan before the Dragon Boat Festival, nearly quadrupling in six months. Breaking down the AI ​​data center, Suzhou's bet isn't just on a few companies, but on a series of interconnected positions. Further upstream are companies like Yuanjie Technology, specializing in optical chips; downstream are companies like Tianfu Communication, specializing in optical devices. Zhongji Xuchuang packages these devices into high-speed optical modules and sends them to data centers worldwide. Lianxun Instruments is responsible for testing whether these high-speed optical communication devices can operate stably. According to Xinhua Daily, the total market capitalization of A-share listed companies in Suzhou has exceeded four trillion yuan, ranking fourth in the country, after Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen. Hangzhou's "Six Little Dragons" appeared on the Spring Festival Gala and trended on social media; Shenzhen's humanoid robots are dancing; and Shanghai's three GPU companies are telling a grand story of domestic substitution. These stories are engaging, novel, and visually appealing; a short video of just a few seconds can make a name memorable. But Suzhou tells a different story. A fiber optic manufacturer in Wujiang saw its sales increase by over 35% year-on-year in 2026, with production capacity already booked until 2027. Multiple companies in Suzhou's industrial chain jointly invested in and started construction of the nation's first 8-inch silicon photonics chip mass production line. Suzhou's optical communication industry has formed a cluster worth hundreds of billions of yuan, with companies leading the establishment of more than 50 innovation consortia. In the first quarter of 2026, the industry's scale grew by over 60%. This wave of AI computing power explosion has first and foremost drawn substantial investment to the "blood vessels" of the industry. For Suzhou, the rise of a leading optical module manufacturer brings more than just market capitalization. It brings with it suppliers, testing equipment manufacturers, optical chip companies, funds, and talent. Its orders translate into production capacity for upstream and downstream companies, its listing enhances the credibility of the local capital market, and its industry assessment becomes a clue for Suzhou's next round of investment attraction. Beyond the paper investment returns, Suzhou gains a continuously growing industrial chain. In 2024, the world suddenly discovered optical modules and rushed into the secondary market to grab shares, but Suzhou's name was already written on the earliest page of Xuchuang's business registration, a long-term shareholder since 2008. Why did Suzhou succeed in its gamble? Some say it was foresight, keen insight, and long-term vision. But these are all hindsight biases. A high vantage point is needed foresight. Suzhou didn't stand on a high pedestal; it crouched down, examining each project and interviewing each person before making its judgment. The person who made that decision for the city was Dai Yu, from Yuanhe Holdings, responsible for the Xuchuang project. Years later, in a media interview, she recalled that her initial contact with Xuchuang came from the park's "Leading Talent Program." A large number of high-end talents returned to China with their projects, and Yuanhe Capital screened them one by one, choosing to invest in Xuchuang. Yuanhe later added more investments through three funds at different stages and a debt platform, round after round, even bringing in Datai Capital from the Dongshahu Fund Town to participate. Restructuring documents show that the Yuanhe system held more than 7% of Xuchuang's equity in total. Yuanhe invested before the 2008 financial crisis and continued until Xuchuang's backdoor listing through Zhongji Equipment in 2017, spanning an entire economic cycle. Around 2011, Xuchuang's 40G products received Google certification, and the company began contacting Amazon and Huawei. In 2014, Google Capital led a $38 million Series C funding round, Google's first investment in China. Accelink originally planned to list on the US stock market, but in 2015, Chinese concept stocks experienced a valuation winter, so it turned back, dismantled its red-chip structure, and sought a way out on the A-share market. In 2017, Zhongji Equipment acquired 100% of Accelink's equity for 2.8 billion yuan, completing a backdoor listing. Wang Weixiu, the actual controller of Zhongji Equipment, decided to boldly delegate power to the Accelink team. New Fortune magazine described this decision as "grand vision." By 2026, Wang Weixiu and his son Wang Xiaodong's net worth had risen to 97.8 billion yuan, jumping from 161st place last year to 30th. The owner of a small company making motor winding equipment, by acquiring a Suzhou-based optical module startup and being willing to delegate power, has seen his net worth approach 100 billion yuan. In 2025, InnoLight Technology's revenue reached 38.2 billion yuan, and its net profit reached 10.8 billion yuan, more than doubling. In the first quarter of 2026, its single-quarter revenue reached 19.5 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 192%, and its net profit reached 5.7 billion yuan, an increase of 262%. Having already established a strong foundation, InnoLight Technology has further invested in a series of companies in the industry chain, including Jingyan Intelligent, Aoke Optoelectronics, and Leixin Optoelectronics, through its subsidiaries. It has also invested in Yuanjie Technology, a new generation of A-share stock market leaders. Looking back at Suzhou's investment record over the past two decades, Xuchuang is not an isolated case. In 2006, "innovative drugs" were not yet a key term in the capital market. Suzhou established BioBAY, which today houses hundreds of pharmaceutical companies and has produced more than thirty listed companies. In the same year, nanotechnology was still far from industrialization, and Suzhou began to attract national-level nanotechnology research institutions. In 2008, the investment promotion team went to Cambridge, England, and brought the voice AI startup, Speechocean, back to Suzhou. That year was a full nine years before the national "New Generation Artificial Intelligence Development Plan" was issued. Biomedicine, nanotechnology, voice AI, and optical modules. Suzhou has invested in all four sectors at a stage when others couldn't yet understand them. Supporting this strategy is a system that, while not particularly appealing, is exceptionally robust. In 2006, Suzhou Industrial Park established the nation's first science and technology investment promotion center. By 2022, all personnel in the investment promotion department held postgraduate degrees or higher, mostly in science and engineering. Suzhou currently has 212 investment promotion agencies and over 2,000 full-time investment promotion personnel. Of these, 119 are state-owned companies, free from formal staffing constraints, employing market-based salaries, and allowing direct equity investment in companies—doing precisely what the government wants to do but cannot achieve through administrative means. In terms of investment judgment, Suzhou has also invented the "Chain Leader Fund," allowing leading companies in the industry chain to act as fund managers, with government funds co-investing. In the biopharmaceutical industry chain master funds, Innovent Biologics and KNJ Biopharma directly serve as managers, participating in project selection and post-investment management. More than ten master funds have cumulatively invested over 20 billion yuan. Therefore, Suzhou's success in this gamble wasn't due to luck, but rather the transformation of "gambling" into a robust system. Using experts to screen projects, veterans of the industry chain to make judgments, and investing with long-term capital of over ten years. This is more than just foresight. This kind of relentless effort, achieved through hardship, is the result of three generations of dedication. In the early days of reform and opening up, Suzhou's industrial foundation was very weak. Equipment was outdated, the structure was backward, and it struggled to secure both raw materials and sales channels. Therefore, it could only do what it could and learn from whomever it could. The "Shanghai Sunday Engineers" were a product of that era. Suzhou's township and village enterprises would invite engineers from large state-owned factories in Shanghai to provide guidance on production during weekends when they were available. By 1988, there were over 15,000 township and village enterprises. In 1992, the output value of township and village industries accounted for more than 70% of the city's total. Even after achieving this, Suzhou didn't get a single opportunity when the state designated special economic zones or established economic and technological development zones. Policy benefits simply didn't come. The people of Kunshan decided to stop waiting. They raised 500,000 yuan themselves, established the first self-funded development zone in the country, assumed all the risks, and went to Shanghai to advertise and attract investment. By adopting a clear-headed yet somewhat humble positioning—"to do the industries Shanghai didn't want to do" and "Shanghai manages everything from zero to one, Suzhou manages everything from one to ten"—Suzhou successfully embedded itself into the Shanghai-dominated industrial chain, completing its initial industrial accumulation. Later, Suzhou learned from Singapore's "door-to-door investment attraction" approach, establishing investment offices globally and proactively contacting companies one by one. By 2012, Suzhou's actual utilization of foreign capital accounted for 8.1% of the national total, a 131-fold increase compared to 1990. Then, production costs rose, environmental capacity reached its limit, and labor-intensive industries began to withdraw. Once again, Suzhou had no choice but to face the growing pains of transformation head-on. Within a 25-square-kilometer area around Nanjing Road in Taicang High-tech Zone, there are over 800 small and medium-sized foreign-invested enterprises, forming a complete automotive industry chain. Suppliers for 70% of the parts for a complete vehicle can be found there. In the Silk Road-Nanhua Cross-border E-commerce Industrial Park in Shengze Town, a 20-story building houses 86 textile companies, with upstream and downstream operations seamlessly integrated from raw materials and fabrics to finished products and sales. These industrial clusters weren't planned; they grew slowly over decades, from individual companies to orders. In the 1980s, he secretly learned the skills of Shanghai engineers on weekends. In the 1990s, he invested 500,000 yuan of his own money to gamble on a development zone. In 2008, he invested 30 million yuan to bet on a niche overseas returnee developing optical modules. When no one believes in its potential, use your own money to buy something that's worthless. This same action has been repeated three times by three generations. Those with foresight choose their path from a high vantage point. Those with no choice squat on the ground, picking up what others don't want, cleaning it, and waiting for it to become valuable. Suzhou's pragmatism is a survival strategy for a prefecture-level city that hasn't obtained a special economic zone license; this strategy, used for thirty years, has become ingrained. When Dai Yu faced Xuchuang's business plan in 2008, the same logic she used to make judgments was the same logic used thirty years earlier when people in Kunshan spent 500,000 yuan. This thing isn't valuable now. But it's worth the gamble. So bet. The flywheel spins. What happens after the gamble is won? An investment turning into a trillion-dollar company is just the first layer of return. It begins to reshape a city's industrial structure. The capital market becomes more active, the industrial chain more complete, AI has real-world manufacturing scenarios, and young people have more reasons to stay. The relationship between the city and the company reverses at this point. In the early years, Suzhou helped companies get started; now that these companies have grown, they become the reason Suzhou continues to attract projects, talent, and capital. Suzhou's figures look impressive. In 2025, its total industrial output above designated size reached 4.89 trillion yuan, ranking second in the country. The output value of high-tech industries increased by 6.7%, accounting for 56.2% of the total, an increase of 1.5 percentage points from the previous year. Twelve companies went public on the A-share market, ranking first in the country and surpassing Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen. One in ten newly listed companies nationwide comes from Suzhou. The per capita disposable income of all residents reached 80,796 yuan, with urban areas exceeding 90,000 yuan, second only to Shanghai, Beijing, and Shenzhen nationwide, and it is only a prefecture-level city. However, among the top ten GDP cities, Suzhou's service sector accounts for only 52.9%, ranking near the bottom. While other major cities have long been transforming towards a service-oriented economy, Suzhou is still desperately manufacturing. Suzhou boasts 160,000 industrial enterprises, encompassing 34 major industrial categories and 514 subcategories. It is one of the cities with the most complete industrial sectors in China and even globally. For industrial AI, this translates to data—high-quality, continuous, and large-scale production data generated daily in workshops. While other cities might begin developing large-scale industrial models by searching for data, Suzhou doesn't need to. Its factories themselves are the data source, and trained models are validated and iterated on the same production line. The production line is both the source of AI learning and the endpoint for AI monetization. Based on a base of 4.89 trillion yuan, even a one or two percentage point increase in efficiency brought by AI corresponds to an absolute increase of tens of billions of yuan per percentage point. Suzhou has also made computing power a public service. The industrial park established a public service platform for computing power. Companies submit their needs, are matched with vendors within 24 hours, sign contracts within a week, and even receive a 20% subsidy. The platform's operations manager described it as like ordering takeout: choose the flavor, portion size, and delivery time, and place the order with one click. For a small, newly established company that can't afford the computing power of large companies, being able to "order takeout" is the difference between survival and death. What do ordinary people gain after the flywheel starts spinning? Let's look at some data. In 2025, 415,000 new urban jobs were created, accounting for nearly 30% of Jiangsu Province's total. Furthermore, 283,000 college graduates stayed in Suzhou. They didn't come because of some genius founder's name; they came because there were real jobs, housing, and a stable life. In the workshops of Bozhong Precision Machinery, AI defect identification has achieved zero missed detections and reduced the over-detection rate of blade batteries to 0.1%. Workers on the production line no longer need to stare at screens with their naked eyes; they have begun to learn how to operate the algorithm system. In the laboratories of Magga Technology, AI intelligent agent clusters have reduced the biomolecule construction cycle from six to ten months to six to eight weeks, freeing up researchers to conduct innovative experiments that they previously had no time for. Efficiency has increased, but people haven't been replaced; instead, they've begun to do more valuable work. Suzhou is currently piloting something interesting: authorizing key enterprises to independently assess talent. You don't need to go to government offices to prove you're worthy of being recruited; if your boss says you're good enough, the government will recognize it. This shows that Suzhou has realized that the person who best understands the value of talent isn't the government, but the person who employs them. This experiment follows the same logic as the Chain Master Fund: giving the judgment power to those who understand best. On the last trading day before the Dragon Boat Festival in June 2026, Zhongji Xuchuang's single-day trading volume exceeded 30 billion yuan. Such a level of liquidity was unimaginable in 2008. Liu Sheng once said, "If Accelink hadn't been able to raise funds before the second half of 2008, this company might not exist." A market capitalization of 1.5 trillion yuan, annual revenue of 38.2 billion yuan, personal wealth of 26.6 billion yuan, an entire industrial chain from optical chips to silicon photonics integration, and countless beams of light running every second in global data centers—all of this started with a man named Dai Yu from a state-owned asset platform in Suzhou. He found Accelink's business plan among a stack of proposals, signed it, and made the payment. In 2026, Suzhou launched eight major initiatives for "AI + Manufacturing." The goal is to exceed 5 trillion yuan in industrial output value above designated size, cultivate 150 large-scale industrial models, and reach a total computing power of 40,000 PFLOPS. The plan, as always, lacks grand vision, a brilliant narrative, and a blockbuster product; it's filled with terms like output value, computing power, and datasets. Perhaps the next investment is already on one of Yuanhe's approval forms. What sector it's investing in, what niche it's betting on, who signed the papers—nobody outside knows. In 1936, a young man named Fei Xiaotong spent two months in Kaixian Gong Village in Wujiang, Suzhou, and later wrote *Jiangcun Economy*, a seminal work in Chinese sociology. What he saw in that village was exactly the same as in Suzhou today: a group of people without first-mover advantage creating their own conditions for development. The silk industry declined, so they tried to set up their own silk reeling factories. Lacking capital, several companies pooled their resources. Lacking technology, they learned from Shanghai. Fei Xiaotong later summarized his life's work in four words: "aiming to enrich the people." When he said this, he probably didn't imagine that ninety years later, in the workshop next to that village, a mere 30 million yuan investment, which no one had high hopes for, would grow into a company worth 1.5 trillion yuan. But he probably wouldn't be surprised either.

data.title: A new way to use stablecoins: "Government bond cashback"
data.description: This could be the beginning of a shift in stablecoins from being "issuer-driven" to "payment network-driven." Open Standard, an independent entity comprised of over 140 financial and technology companies including Stripe, Visa, BlackRock, and Coinbase, announced the launch of its USD stablecoin project, Open USD (OUSD), with plans to complete on-chain deployment on networks such as Solana, Stellar, Base, and Polygon by the end of 2026. Zach Abrams, co-founder of Bridge, the stablecoin infrastructure company previously acquired by Stripe for $1.1 billion, will serve as the first CEO of Open Standard. From the perspective of the participants, OUSD's supporters cover payment networks, traditional banks, asset management institutions, crypto platforms, and large technology companies, bringing almost all the key players needed for stablecoins to enter the mainstream payment system into the same alliance. The project will be governed by a board of directors composed of its members, attempting to establish a completely new profit-sharing structure among issuance, distribution, and use cases. This initial alliance, which can be described as half of the traditional financial and crypto worlds, is based on the core logic of decentralizing the nominal issuer and shifting the trillion-dollar interest pool previously monopolized by Circle and Tether towards the distribution side that actually contributes transaction volume. Currently, Tether dominates the market with a circulating supply of USDT of $185 billion, while Circle holds second place with a circulating supply of USDC of approximately $75 billion. The interest income from their reserves constitutes the main body of this interest pool. The design mechanism of OUSD can be summarized in three points: zero-cost minting and redemption, returning reserve returns to partners after deducting management expenses, and a board of directors jointly governed by partners. As the nominal issuer, Open Standard does not retain the main interest income. Instead, it distributes revenue to distributors who actually contribute to the scale of usage based on the transaction volume and holdings of participating companies. In other words, the core change lies in revenue attribution: from centralized acquisition by the issuer to distribution based on distribution contribution. In the traditional stablecoin model, the issuer's profit logic is closer to a bank's net interest margin: users and payment platforms provide the circulation volume (similar to interest-free deposits), the issuer is responsible for allocating the underlying reserve assets, and obtains the main interest income. OUSD attempts to adjust this structure. By incorporating distribution partners into the revenue sharing system, the reserve returns, originally concentrated in the issuer's hands, are now flowing back to channels that generate actual usage, based on transaction volume and holding balances. For platforms like Stripe, Shopify, and Visa, this means that choosing a stablecoin is no longer just a matter of technology or clearing efficiency, but also begins to impact their revenue structure. By channeling their transaction volume and merchant funds into OUSD, they have the opportunity to participate in the interest rate spread that was previously exclusively enjoyed by the issuer. This default mechanism of shared reserve returns adds an economic layer to the evaluation of whether traditional enterprises should adopt stablecoins. In contrast, in the existing ecosystem dominated by Circle and Tether, issuers typically retain all reserve interest. Regardless of the transaction volume contributed by Stripe, Visa, or other merchants, most remain at the usage level and have difficulty participating in revenue sharing. Previously, platforms chose USDC primarily based on its instrumental attributes such as liquidity, settlement efficiency, and ecosystem maturity. OUSD aims to change this revenue structure. Within this framework, distribution channels will have clearer economic incentives. By directing transaction traffic to OUSD, distributors can share in the returns of reserve assets, making stablecoins not only payment channels but also financial instruments with profit-sharing attributes. Platforms that control merchant networks and transaction entry points thus gain the opportunity to participate in the underlying interest distribution. It is this incentive mechanism that makes distributors more proactive in driving adoption. Traditional stablecoins often rely on expanding their merchant base and channels one by one, while OUSD integrates multi-party distribution capabilities from the outset through an alliance model. This means that the distribution network itself becomes one of its key advantages, even though some mechanisms are still under development. Over 140 initial partners provide a distribution foundation covering both crypto and traditional finance, and the combination of different members corresponds to a large number of potential B2B payment scenarios. At the same time, the alliance includes both large payment companies and mainstream crypto exchanges, giving it strong deposit and withdrawal capabilities from the outset, which is one of the key conditions for stablecoins to enter the actual payment system. Essentially, this is more like a profit-sharing stablecoin structure designed for B2B distribution networks. The stablecoin market has generally followed two paths: centralized issuance and concentrated returns, represented by USDT and USDC, and decentralized solutions that emphasize decentralized governance but are weaker in distribution. OUSD attempts to find a balance between the two: maintaining the efficiency of centralized clearing while opening up the distribution network to profit sharing. While OUSD is nominally a decentralized stablecoin issuance, its profit structure resembles a collective negotiation initiated by a channel alliance regarding the issuer's interest rate spread. Therefore, it is understandable that the three largest stablecoin issuers—Circle, Tether, and PayPal—did not participate. For the issuers who are bypassed, the pressure from this model has already been reflected in the capital markets. Circle (NYSE: CRCL), a representative of US stock stablecoins, saw its stock price plummet by 17.5% that day, partly due to passive selling caused by the FTSE Russell semi-annual index restructuring. Circle was removed from several growth stock indices during the same period, triggering mechanical selling by tracking funds. However, even excluding this factor, the market's reaction to the OUSD news itself still points to a reassessment of the sustainability of the old model where issuers concentrate their profits. For Circle, how much liquidity OUSD can steal in the short term may not be the most important thing. After all, what's hardest for stablecoins to replace is the inertia built up by liquidity, trust, and compliance. However, the real issue of repricing reserve yield distribution is what's causing the most anxiety in the market and is also the most vulnerable part of Circle's business model. This vulnerability is primarily reflected in its revenue structure. Circle's revenue is highly dependent on interest rates. In 2025, its total revenue was $2.75 billion, the vast majority of which came from interest income on USDC reserve assets. Reserve income in the fourth quarter alone reached $733 million, accounting for over 95% of total revenue. The dollars deposited by users into USDC are used to buy short-term US Treasury bonds, and Circle earns its profits from the interest on these bonds. Circle also needs to pay a significant percentage of revenue to its core distributors. According to Circle's IPO prospectus and financial reports, Coinbase takes all the reserve income generated from USDC held on the Coinbase platform, while reserve income generated from other channels is split 50/50 between the two companies. In 2024, Circle paid Coinbase $908 million in distribution fees, accounting for approximately 54% of its total revenue. This means that for every $1 Circle earned, more than half was paid to Coinbase as distribution fees; and Coinbase itself neither issues USDC nor assumes reserve management responsibility. This revenue-sharing arrangement is difficult for Circle to unilaterally adjust. According to disclosures, this distribution agreement cannot be unilaterally terminated by Circle and automatically renews every three years, meaning it doesn't have complete control over pricing and negotiation. Whether future profit margins can improve largely depends on Circle's ability to establish more diversified distribution channels outside of Coinbase and reduce its dependence on a single core partner. The emergence of OUSD essentially aims to redistribute stablecoin reserve returns from issuers to distribution networks that control transaction volume and funding channels. This list of top partners, coupled with Stripe's extensive financial product line, gives this challenge real weight. Circle faces not only the challenge of maintaining USDC's size, but also the challenge of retaining control over reserve return distribution. It will be worth watching whether Circle adjusts its distribution strategy and partnership structure, and how other issuers and distributors who have not yet clearly aligned themselves will respond to this change. Conclusion: Challenges to the Implementation of OUSD OUSD's distribution network and deposit/withdrawal capabilities are its biggest potential advantages and a key link for stablecoins to transition from crypto-native tools to mainstream payment systems. However, whether this advantage can be realized depends on whether these channels can effectively migrate real business traffic such as default settlement paths, merchant acquiring, and cross-border fund pools. Meanwhile, reserve asset allocation, cross-regional liquidity arrangements, and multi-jurisdictional compliance frameworks still lack clear answers. More subtly, the internal interests within the alliance are also a factor. Coinbase remains deeply involved in and benefits from the USDC ecosystem, yet it joined OUSD as a founding member at this time, which is perhaps more noteworthy than the list of 140 companies itself. Coinbase and Circle's USDC revenue-sharing agreement has its first three-year renewal window expire in August 2026, less than two months after the OUSD alliance was announced. This timing makes OUSD not just a new stablecoin option, but also seems like an additional bargaining chip for Coinbase before renewal negotiations. Large channel providers clearly don't intend to bet on just one stablecoin; they value maintaining bargaining power among different issuers and stablecoin networks. Whether OUSD can ultimately achieve economies of scale depends on whether participants substantially drive traffic or continue to bet on multiple fronts. This is still uncertain. But regardless of the outcome, this alliance has broken an important assumption: that reserve returns are exclusively enjoyed by the issuer is not a natural rule of the stablecoin business model. In this sense, Open Standard is more like a business-level attempt at decentralization, pushing the profit margins that were originally concentrated in the hands of issuers like Circle and Tether to the platform that truly brings in trading volume and balance accumulation. The center hasn't disappeared; it's just that the issuer is no longer the sole and obvious recipient of the profits.

data.title: CZ: Competitor attacks are "too naive"; Greece's visa refusal is a lose-lose situation.
data.description:
Source: The Starting Block: CZ Interview

Translator: CryptoLeo; Editor: Qin Xiaofeng

Ten years is too long, seize the day. I hope people remember me as a "Crypto Guy."

Editor's Note: Binance founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ) recently gave an interview to The Block blog in Dubai, where he responded candidly to sensitive topics such as the inside story of Musk's $500 million investment, the Greek regulatory turmoil, and his experience surviving in prison.

He frankly admitted to a past where he "would rather ask for forgiveness than permission," reflecting that if he could do it again, he would "directly block all American users"; he asserted that crypto is the future of currency, predicting that Bitcoin may reach one million dollars by 2033; he revealed that his most frequently used AI is the free version of Kimi, simply because he "ran out of his paid quota." From a workaholic who worked 21 hours a day to a "seize the day" industry promoter, CZ's transformation and perseverance are all revealed in this exclusive interview.

I. Ten years is too long, seize the day, crypto is still the core and the future

Q: Let's talk about you and Musk first. You've never met him, but you invested $500 million when he acquired X. Why spend so much money?

Q: Let's talk about you and Musk first. You've never met him, but you invested $500 million when he acquired X.

CZ: To achieve financial freedom, you first need freedom of speech, and X is a very important platform for freedom of speech. While $500 million is a lot, it's only about 1% of Musk's $44 billion acquisition price. I'm glad that cryptocurrency companies can contribute to freedom of speech. This investment went through some twists and turns. I originally thought the deal would be completed in May 2022, but it was ultimately completed in October. At that time, many people said X wasn't worth $44 billion. I felt we should support capable entrepreneurs, and the result was quite good. Q: Why haven't you met with Musk? Were there opportunities but no success? CZ: We didn't deliberately avoid each other; our schedules are both quite busy. Even regarding that $500 million investment, I only exchanged about a dozen messages with him. On average, we probably only exchange a few messages a year. We're both very busy, though I'm not as busy now. I think our idea is: we only meet when necessary. Q: I think X's development over the past year has been very interesting. One trend is X's native payment system. What do you think the future of social payments will be like? CZ: Most payment solutions are limited to specific countries, such as Stripe and PayPal in the US; and WeChat and Alipay in China, all of which are country-specific. I hope X can overcome this. Cryptocurrency may be the first payment technology to break down national borders. I did speak with Musk when I learned that X announced X money. I sent Musk a message on X: Can we become partners? He said X money handles all fiat currencies, so they won't touch cryptocurrencies, but I do hope X can become a global platform—currency should be global, and we have no reason to divide currencies by country. For example, you can have your own monetary policy, but you're using a global currency. I really hope X can do that. And crypto won't take away a piece of the existing payment model's "cake"—we all hope for better services that allow the industry to grow larger. Humans should be able to transact and do business globally. Q: Speaking of you and Musk, Musk is probably the most influential person in the world. Would you like to become the most influential person in the crypto space? What do you hope people will remember you for in the future? Or something else? CZ: Wealth rankings aren't important. I don't want to compete with Musk or anyone else, and I don't really care how I'll be remembered. Regarding this question, I hope that when I look back on my life before I die, I can say to myself, "I've done my best." I always say that, which also means that if I could, I should have done more. So I'm currently in a phase where I'm helping other founders; not leading, but being a enabler. I think Musk is amazing; he's driven so many incredibly difficult companies and projects, and he deserves to be the richest person in the world. I'm different from him in this respect. Binance is a lucky and successful platform; we've been able to help over 300 million users globally, and many users write me thank-you notes—that's a great achievement.
